1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
8	import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.WeilDivisor
9	
10	/-!
11	# The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point and its global sections (RR.3)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.3** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)` and the
22	  degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and the Riemann–Roch dimension formula
25	  `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0`.
26	- **`RR.3` (this file)**: the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point
27	  `P ∈ C`, its `k̄`-vector space of global sections as the
28	  Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`, the `H¹`-vanishing
29	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a genus-`0` curve, the dimension formula
30	  `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`, and the existence of a non-constant
31	  rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with at most a simple pole at `P`.
32	- `RR.4` (`RationalCurveIso.lean`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34	
35	## Status (iter-183 Lane A — sig amend + carrierSet scaffold)
36

112	/-! ## §1. The line bundle of a closed point on a smooth proper curve
113	
114	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a closed
115	point `P ∈ C`, the local ring `𝒪_{C,P}` is a DVR with maximal ideal
116	generated by a uniformiser `f_P`. Hartshorne's construction `ℒ(D)`
117	(II §6 p. 144) applied to the Cartier divisor `[P]` (locally cut out by
118	`f_P` near `P`, by `1` elsewhere) produces the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)`:
119	the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)`
120	generated locally by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on `C \ {P}`. -/
121	
122	namespace Scheme
123	
124	/-! ### Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` carrier (iter-183 Lane A scaffold)
125	
126	The substantive iter-183 contribution: a concrete per-open `Set`-valued
127	carrier of the line bundle `𝒪_C(P)`, realised directly as Hartshorne's
128	subsheaf of the function-field constant sheaf `K_C` (Hartshorne II §6
129	p. 144; analogist `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md` Decision 3
130	`ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`).
131	
132	A section of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U` is a rational function `f ∈ K(C)`
133	satisfying the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
134	`Q ≠ P` with `Q.point ∈ U` (regularity on the complement of `P` inside
135	`U`) and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
136	The construction is independent of the choice of uniformiser at `P`:
137	any two uniformisers differ by a unit, so the order-`≥ −1` condition at
138	`P` is intrinsic.
139	
140	iter-184+ will upgrade `carrierSet` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the
141	closure proofs (zero / addition / kbar-scalar), bundle the result as a
142	presheaf functor (identity-on-`K(C)` restrictions, monotone in `U` via
143	`carrierSet_mono`), and discharge the sheaf property via gluing-by-
144	stalks (stalk-locality of the order conditions at each prime divisor). -/
145	
146	/-- **Carrier set** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U : (Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`: the
147	set of rational functions `f ∈ K(C)` satisfying the order conditions
148	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q` with `Q.point ∈ U.unop`,
149	`Q.point ≠ P`, and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U.unop`.
150	
151	iter-183 Lane A landed this as a concrete substantive `Set` definition;
152	no sorry. -/
153	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet
154	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
155	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
156	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
157	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
158	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
159	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
160	    Set C.left.functionField := by
161	  let Phat : C.left.PrimeDivisor := ⟨P, hPcoh⟩
162	  haveI := Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
163	    (X := C.left) Phat
164	  exact { f | (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ∈ U.unop.1 → Q.point ≠ P →
165	              0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
166	              (P ∈ U.unop.1 → (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Phat f) }
167	
168	/-- **Monotonicity of `carrierSet` in `U`**: when `V.unop ⊆ U.unop` (i.e.
169	the open `V` is contained in the open `U`), the carrier on `U` is
170	INCLUDED in the carrier on `V` (the order conditions over the smaller
171	open `V` involve fewer prime divisors, hence are easier to satisfy).
172	
173	This is the substantive monotonicity that drives the (identity-on-`K(C)`)
174	restriction map of the would-be `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf` functor:
175	in `(Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`, an arrow `U ⟶ V` corresponds to `V.unop ⊆ U.unop`,
176	and the restriction map `carrierSet U → carrierSet V` is the inclusion
177	delivered by this lemma.
178	
179	iter-183 Lane A landed this as the substantive monotonicity proof; no
180	sorry. -/
181	private lemma lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet_mono
182	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
183	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
184	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
185	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
186	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
187	    {U V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ}
188	    (hUV : V.unop.1 ⊆ U.unop.1) :
189	    lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
190	      ⊆ lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh V := by
191	  intro f hf
192	  refine ⟨fun Q hQV hQP => hf.1 Q (hUV hQV) hQP, fun hPV => hf.2 (hUV hPV)⟩
193	
194	/-- **Carrier submodule** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U`: upgrade of
195	`carrierSet U` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the three closure proofs
196	(`0`, `+`, `kbar • _`). The closure proofs rest on the DVR-shipped
197	`Ring.ordFrac_add` (non-archimedean inequality on the discrete valuation
198	of a regular-in-codim-1 stalk; iter-186 Step 1 upgraded the carrier class
199	so this is invocable) and `Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit` (the scalar from
200	`kbar` becomes a unit in the stalk, preserving the order under scalar
201	multiplication).
202	
203	iter-186 Step 2 (per analogist `ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
204	Decision 2): structural skeleton with the load-bearing Mathlib lemmas in
205	place; bookkeeping `sorry`s remain for the prover phase. -/
206	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule
207	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
208	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
209	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
210	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
211	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
212	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
213	    Submodule kbar C.left.functionField where
214	  carrier := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
215	  zero_mem' := by
216	    -- `order Y 0 = WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0`;
217	    -- both `0 ≤ 0` and `-1 ≤ 0` hold trivially.
218	    sorry
219	  add_mem' := by
220	    -- Case-split on `f + g = 0` (trivial via `WithZero.log_zero`), else
221	    -- apply `Ring.ordFrac_add` (DVR-shipped, Step 1 unlocks) and
222	    -- `WithZero.log` monotonicity on the nonzero part.
223	    sorry
224	  smul_mem' := by
225	    -- For `c : kbar`, the scalar action `c • f` on `K(C)` factors through
226	    -- `algebraMap kbar K(C)`. For `c ≠ 0`, `algebraMap kbar K(C) c` is a
227	    -- unit in `K(C)` (since `kbar` is a field), so the order under
228	    -- multiplication is preserved by `Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit` +
229	    -- multiplicativity of `Ring.ordFrac`. For `c = 0`, the result reduces
230	    -- to `zero_mem'`.
231	    sorry

304	/-! ## §2. Global sections as the Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`
305	
306	Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7 identifies the global sections of
307	`ℒ(D_0)` with the rational functions `f ∈ K(X)^×` satisfying
308	`div(f) ≥ −D_0` (plus the zero section). Specialised to `D_0 = [P]` on a
309	curve, the condition `div(f) + [P] ≥ 0` rewrites coordinate-wise as
310	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`. -/

314	Given `f ∈ K(C)^×` with `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P`
315	and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`, the rational function `f` lifts to a global section
316	`s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under the canonical inclusion
317	`𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f`. Hartshorne's construction inside the
318	proof of Proposition 7.7(b) (p. 157) defines this section locally on the
319	affine cover witnessing the Cartier-divisor structure of `[P]`: on a
320	neighbourhood `U_i` of `P` the section is `f · f_P` (in
321	`𝒪_C(P)(U_i) = f_P⁻¹ · 𝒪_C(U_i)`, this is `(f · f_P) · f_P⁻¹ = f`);
322	on the complement `C ∖ {P}` the section is `f` directly (the order
323	conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` guarantee `f ∈ 𝒪_C(U) = 𝒪_C(P)(U)`
324	on any affine open `U ⊆ C ∖ {P}`).

412	* the order conditions hold: `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
413	  `Q ∈ C.PrimeDivisor` whose generic point is not `P`, and
414	  `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (where the latter is read off the prime divisor
415	  `⟨P, h⟩` with `h : Order.coheight P = 1` the codimension-one witness
416	  automatic for a closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme).
417	
418	The iff is the substantive content of Hartshorne's Proposition 7.7(b) /
419	its proof, specialised to `D_0 = [P]`.
420	
421	**iter-181 Lane A PARTIAL — directional split landed**: the iff is now

440	lemma globalSections_iff
441	    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
442	        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
443	    (P : C.left) (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
444	    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
445	    (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :

447	        0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
448	      (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ f
449	    ↔

451	        (lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP hPcoh) 0,
452	      lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField
453	        (C := C) P hP hPcoh s = f :=

455	   globalSections_iff_mpr P hP f hf hPcoh⟩

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	\begin{definition}
102	
103	
104	\leanok
105	[Line bundle of a closed point]
106	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
108	  
109	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
110	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
111	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
113	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
114	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
115	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
116	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
117	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
118	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
119	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
120	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
121	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
122	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
123	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
124	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
125	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
126	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
127	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
128	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
129	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
130	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
131	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
132	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
133	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
134	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
135	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
136	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
137	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
138	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
139	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
140	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
141	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
142	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
143	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
144	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
145	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
146	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
147	
148	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
149	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
150	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
151	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
152	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
153	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
154	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
155	  \[
156	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
157	    \mathcal O_C)
158	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
159	  \]
160	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
161	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
162	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
163	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
164	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
165	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
166	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
167	
168	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
169	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
170	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
171	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
172	  REF), and returns an invertible
173	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
174	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
175	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
176	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
177	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
178	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
179	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
180	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}
182	
183	\begin{definition}
184	
185	\leanok
186	[Inclusion of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into the function field]
187	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
188	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
189	  
190	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
191	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\); the
192	  % canonical inclusion \(\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K\) is part
193	  % of the construction) (read from
194	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
195	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
196	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
197	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
198	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
199	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
200	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
201	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
202	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
203	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
204	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
205	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
206	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
207	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
208	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient
209	  rings \(\mathcal K_C\). Since \(C\) is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the
210	  constant sheaf at the function field \(K(C)\), and taking global sections
211	  of the structural inclusion \(\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\)
212	  produces a canonical \(\bar k\)-linear map
213	  \[
214	    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
215	      \;\longrightarrow\; H^0(C, \mathcal K_C) \;\cong\; K(C)
216	  \]
217	  sending a global section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to the rational
218	  function on \(C\) obtained by composing \(s\) with the inclusion of
219	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into \(\mathcal K_C\) at each point. The map \(\iota\) is
220	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
221	  \mathcal K_C\( is torsion-free of rank \)1$, so taking global sections
222	  preserves injectivity on the integral scheme \(C\)).
223	
224	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
225	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
226	  takes the same inputs as REF (a curve
227	  \(C\), a point \(P\), and the closedness witness
228	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C.left)}) and produces, for each global
229	  section \(s\) of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) at twist \(0\) in the project's
230	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured global-sections pipeline, the corresponding
231	  element \(\iota(s) \in K(C)\) of the project's function-field carrier
232	  \texttt{C.left.functionField}. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}
233	  and its body is gated on the body of
234	  REF.
235	\end{definition}

237	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
238	
239	\begin{lemma}
240	
241	
242	\leanok
243	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
244	controlled pole]
245	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
247	  
248	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
249	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
250	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
251	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
252	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
253	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
255	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
256	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
257	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
258	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
259	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
260	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
261	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
262	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
263	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
264	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
265	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
266	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
267	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
268	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
269	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
270	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
271	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
272	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
273	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
274	  Riemann--Roch space
275	  \[
276	    L([P]) \;:=\;
277	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
278	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
279	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
280	  \]
281	  Equivalently, the order conditions
282	  \[
283	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
284	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
285	    \qquad
286	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
287	  \]
288	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
289	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
290	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
291	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
292	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
293	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
294	
295	  Under this identification, the inclusion
296	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
297	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
298	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
299	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
300	\end{lemma}
301	
302	\begin{proof}
303	  
304	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
305	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
306	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
307	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
308	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
309	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
310	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
311	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
312	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
313	  and only if:
314	
315	  \begin{itemize}
316	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
317	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
318	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
319	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
320	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
321	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
322	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
323	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
324	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
325	  \end{itemize}
326	  In divisorial language (REF,
327	  REF), the two conditions combine to
328	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
329	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
330	  zero global section.
331	
332	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
333	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
334	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
335	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
336	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
337	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
338	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
339	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
340	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
341	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
342	\end{proof}

344	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}
347	
348	
349	\leanok
350	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
351	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
353	  
354	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
355	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
356	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
357	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
358	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
359	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
360	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
361	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
362	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
363	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
364	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
365	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
366	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
367	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
368	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
369	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
370	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
371	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
372	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
373	  \[
374	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
375	  \]
376	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
377	\end{lemma}
378	
379	\begin{proof}
380	  
381	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
382	  exact sequence
383	  \[
384	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
385	  \]
386	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
387	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
388	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
389	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
390	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
391	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
392	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
393	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
394	  \[
395	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
396	  \]
397	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
398	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
399	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
400	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
401	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
402	  \begin{multline*}
403	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
404	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
405	  \end{multline*}
406	  Two ingredients close the proof:
407	
408	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
409	  hypothesis: by REF,
410	  \[
411	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
412	  \]
413	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
414	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
415	
416	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
417	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
418	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
419	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
420	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
421	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
422	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
423	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
424	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
425	
426	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
427	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
428	  \[
429	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
430	  \]
431	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
432	
433	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
434	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
435	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
436	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
437	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
438	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
439	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
440	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
441	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
442	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
443	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
444	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
445	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
446	  complex degenerates).
447	\end{proof}
448	
449	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
450	
451	\begin{theorem}
452	
453	
454	\leanok
455	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
456	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
457	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
458	  
459	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
460	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
461	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
462	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
463	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
464	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
465	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
466	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
467	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
468	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
469	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
470	  % is the Euler characteristic
471	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
472	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
473	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
474	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
475	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
476	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
477	  \[
478	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
479	  \]
480	\end{theorem}
481	
482	\begin{proof}
483	  
484	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
485	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
486	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
487	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
488	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
489	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
490	  \[
491	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
492	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
493	  \]
494	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
495	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
496	  \[
497	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
498	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
499	    \;=\; 2.
500	  \]
501	  By
502	  REF, the second
503	  term vanishes:
504	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
505	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
506	
507	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
508	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
509	  REF computes
510	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
511	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
512	  \[
513	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
514	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
515	  \]
516	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
517	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
518	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
519	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
520	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
521	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
522	  segment yields the short exact sequence
523	  \[
524	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
525	  \]
526	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
527	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
528	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
529	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
530	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
531	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
532	\end{proof}
533	
534	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
535	
536	\begin{corollary}
537	
538	
539	\leanok
540	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
541	single simple pole]
542	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
543	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
544	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
545	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
549	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
550	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
551	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
552	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
554	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
555	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
556	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
557	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
558	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
559	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
560	  \begin{itemize}
561	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
562	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
563	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
564	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
565	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
566	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
567	  \end{itemize}
568	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
569	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
570	\end{corollary}
571	
572	\begin{proof}
573	  
574	  By
575	  REF,
576	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
577	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
578	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
579	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
580	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
581	  identification of
582	  REF), is a one-dimensional
583	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
584	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
585	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
586	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
587	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
588	
589	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
590	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
591	  REF the section
592	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
593	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
594	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
595	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
596	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
597	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
598	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
599	  of the statement are now immediate.
600	
601	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
602	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
603	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
604	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
605	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
606	
607	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
608	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
609	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
610	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
611	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
612	  REF's output. The
613	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
614	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
615	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
616	\end{proof}
617	
618	\section{Mathlib-status flag: the H\(^1\)-vanishing input}
619	
620	The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve
621	(REF) is the
622	critical cohomological input of this chapter. Its closure depends on
623	two Mathlib-status facts, both of which the project already consumes
624	elsewhere:
625	
626	\begin{itemize}
627	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence of \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf
628	    cohomology.} Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships
629	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact} for
630	    \(\Ext^*\)-long-exact-sequences at the abelian-group level. The
631	    project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured pipeline
632	    (REF, \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex})
633	    inherits it by forget-functor naturality. This bridge is already
634	    consumed by REF of
635	    \texttt{RR.2}, so closing it here introduces no new Mathlib
636	    obligation.
637	  \item \textbf{Vanishing of higher cohomology of a skyscraper sheaf}
638	    on a Noetherian scheme. Hartshorne~III.2.5 gives the flasque
639	    formulation; the project's affine-cover Čech route
640	    (REF and surrounding
641	    \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex} machinery) computes
642	    the same vanishing for the skyscraper at a closed point by direct
643	    Čech-degeneration. \texttt{RR.2}'s inductive step already invokes
644	    \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), which is the combined statement
645	    \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and \(\dim H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\),
646	    so the vanishing \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) is already a consumer
647	    obligation of \texttt{RR.2}.
648	\end{itemize}
649	
650	Consequently the H\(^1\)-vanishing
651	REF introduces no
652	Mathlib obligation that is not already on \texttt{RR.2}'s critical
653	path. The genus-\(0\) specialisation should therefore close at the
654	formalisation stage without splitting this chapter or pushing the
655	vanishing back into \texttt{RR.2}.
656	
657	% NOTE (iter-174 plan-agent flag). The general-\(g\) vanishing statement
658	% ``\(H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0\) whenever \(\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2\)''
659	% (Hartshorne~IV.1, the Riemann--Roch consequence read covariantly) is
660	% \emph{not} a deliverable of this chapter and is not required for the
661	% genus-\(0\) critical path: the genus-\(0\) argument closes via the long
662	% exact sequence at \(D = 0\) without ever needing the general bound. The
663	% general bound is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement and is queued for
664	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter together with the dualizing sheaf
665	% \(\omega_C\) and the canonical-divisor identification \(K_C \sim -2[P]\)
666	% on \(\mathbb P^1\) (Hartshorne~II.8.20.1).
667	
668	\section{Out of scope}
669	
670	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.3}
671	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters or queued for
672	follow-up iterations:
673	
674	\begin{itemize}
675	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
676	    associated to an arbitrary Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
677	    \mathrm{Div}(C)$, and the linear-equivalence isomorphism
678	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), are
679	    \emph{not} performed in this chapter. The chapter constructs only
680	    the specialisation \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) a prime divisor,
681	    which is the only case consumed by the genus-\(0\) classification
682	    chain. The general \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is queued for the
683	    post-RR-bridge follow-up (where it pairs with the general-\(g\)
684	    Riemann--Roch formula and the canonical-divisor identification).
685	  \item The general-\(g\) statement
686	    $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = \deg([P]) + 1 -
687	    g(C) + \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, while a direct
688	    consequence of
689	    REF of \texttt{RR.2},
690	    is not separately formalised here; the chapter restricts to the
691	    genus-\(0\) specialisation \(g = 0\) on which
692	    REF is
693	    available.
694	  \item \textbf{Serre duality} $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong
695	    H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$ and the associated
696	    canonical divisor \(K_C = c_1(\omega_C)\) are \emph{not} used at any
697	    step of this chapter and are not deliverables; the chapter routes
698	    cohomological vanishing through the long exact sequence at
699	    \(D = 0\) (REF)
700	    instead. Serre duality is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
701	  \item The morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) produced by applying
702	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} to the basis \((1, f)\) of
703	    REF,
704	    and its identification as an isomorphism, are the content of
705	    \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
706	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The present
707	    chapter supplies the basis \((1, f)\) and stops.
708	  \item The general-\(g\) vanishing
709	    \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0\) for \(\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2\) is
710	    \emph{not} part of this chapter and is not required for the
711	    genus-\(0\) critical path. It is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement
712	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
713	  \item The interpretation of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as the space
714	    of sections of a complete linear system \(|[P]|\) in
715	    projective-space form (Hartshorne~II.7.7) is gestured at by
716	    REF but the
717	    projective-space structure on the underlying set is not separately
718	    formalised; the chapter consumes only the \(\bar k\)-vector space
719	    structure on \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
720	\end{itemize}
